explanation: The compiler hit something it didn't expect and its best guess is that a semicolon is missing on the line above. Check the end of the previous statement.

match: expected declaration or statement at end of input
explanation: The compiler ran out of file before it found a closing '}'. Count your braces; one of them is missing.
Your braces don't balance. Somewhere a '{' was opened that never got a matching '}', or vice versa.
You called a function the compiler hasn't seen declared yet, usually a missing #include, or the function is defined further down the file than where you called it.
You declared this variable but never read its value. Not an error, but usually a sign of a bug: either you meant to use it, or you can delete it.

match: control reaches end of non-void function
explanation: This function promises to return a value but there's a path through it that doesn't hit a return statement.

match: assignment makes pointer from integer without a cast
explanation: You're assigning a plain number to a pointer variable. This is almost always a bug. Did you mean to use & to take an address, or forget a dereference *?
You're comparing a pointer to a plain number. If you meant to check for a null pointer, compare against NULL (or 0) explicitly and make sure both sides are actually pointers.
The compiler has never seen this name before at this point in the file. Common causes: a typo, using a variable outside the { } block it was declared in, or using it before its declaration.
You've declared or defined this function/variable twice with two different types. Check every declaration matches exactly.
The compiler expected a value or expression here and found something else instead, often a stray operator, an extra comma, or a misplaced symbol.
This name has already been defined earlier in the file (or in an included header). Two functions or variables can't share the same name in the same scope.
You're assigning or passing a pointer of one type where a pointer of a different type is expected. Check the types on both sides carefully.
You called this function with fewer arguments than it's declared to take. Check the function's signature.
You called this function with more arguments than it's declared to take. Check the function's signature.
You defined a function without giving it a return type. Modern C requires every function to declare one explicitly (even 'void' if it returns nothing).
A printf/scanf-family format specifier (like %d or %s) doesn't match the type of the argument you passed. Double-check every %X against the variable next to it, in order.
The compiler expected a name (a variable, function, or type) here and found a symbol instead, often a missing type keyword or a typo.
